beginner.question: What is an HTML element?
beginner.answer: An HTML element is the basic building block of a web page. It consists of an opening tag, optional attributes, content, and a closing tag.

beginner.question: What is the difference between an opening tag and a closing tag?
beginner.answer: An opening tag is written as <tagname>. A closing tag is written as </tagname> with a forward slash before the tag name.

beginner.question: What is a void element? Give three examples.
beginner.answer:
A void element has no content and no closing tag. Examples:
(line break),
(horizontal rule), (image).

beginner.question: What is the difference between block-level and inline elements?
beginner.answer: Block-level elements start on a new line and take up the full width. Inline elements stay on the same line and only take up as much width as their content.

beginner.question:
What does the

element do?
beginner.answer:
The

element defines a paragraph. It is a block-level element that creates a block of text with spacing above and below.

beginner.question:
What is the
element used for?
beginner.answer:
The
element creates a line break within text or content, moving subsequent content to the next line.

beginner.question: What does the <hr> element represent?
beginner.answer: The <hr> element represents a thematic break in content, often displayed as a horizontal line.

beginner.question: What is the <div> element?
beginner.answer: The <div> is a generic block-level container with no semantic meaning, used for grouping and styling purposes.

beginner.question: What is the <span> element?
beginner.answer: The <span> is a generic inline container with no semantic meaning, used for styling specific portions of text.

beginner.question: What does the element do?
beginner.answer: The element embeds an image in the page. It requires the src attribute for the image URL and the alt attribute for alternative text.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <strong> and <b>?
intermediate.answer: <strong> has semantic meaning — it indicates strong importance and can affect screen reader emphasis. <b> is purely presentational (bold text) with no semantic meaning. Use <strong> for importance, <b> for stylistic bold.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <em> and <i>?
intermediate.answer: <em> has semantic meaning — it indicates emphasis and affects how screen readers pronounce the text. <i> is purely presentational (italic text). Use <em> for emphasis, <i> for technical terms, foreign words, or thoughts.

intermediate.question: What elements can the <a> element contain in HTML5?
intermediate.answer:
In HTML5, the element can contain block-level elements, including
,

-

,

, and even entire sections, making whole cards clickable.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the <figure> and <figcaption> elements?
intermediate.answer: <figure> represents self-contained content like images, diagrams, or code snippets. <figcaption> provides a caption for the <figure> content.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <article> and <section>?
intermediate.answer: <article> contains self-contained, independently distributable content (like a blog post). <section> groups related content thematically within a page.

intermediate.question: What is the <aside> element used for?
intermediate.answer: <aside> represents content indirectly related to the main content, such as sidebars, pull quotes, or advertising. It should be tangentially related.

intermediate.question: How do table elements (<thead>, <tbody>, <tfoot>) improve structure?
intermediate.answer: They divide tables into semantic sections: <thead> for header rows, <tbody> for data rows, and <tfoot> for footer summaries. This improves accessibility and styling.

intermediate.question: What is the <details> and <summary> element used for?
intermediate.answer: <details> creates a disclosure widget that can be toggled open/closed. <summary> provides the visible label. Users can click to reveal or hide additional content.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the <abbr> element?
intermediate.answer: <abbr> marks up abbreviations or acronyms. The title attribute can provide the full expansion, which appears as a tooltip on hover.

intermediate.question: What is the <code> element used for?
intermediate.answer: <code> marks up a fragment of computer code. It is typically displayed in a monospace font. For multi-line code, use <pre> with <code>.

advanced.question: Explain HTML5 content categories. How do they affect element placement?
advanced.answer: HTML5 defines categories: Metadata content (head elements), Flow content (body content), Sectioning content (creates sections), Heading content (titles), Phrasing content (text-level), Embedded content (external resources), and Interactive content (user interaction). These categories determine valid parent-child relationships.

advanced.question: What is the difference between sectioning content and heading content in the HTML5 outline algorithm?
advanced.answer: Sectioning content (<article>, <section>, <nav>, <aside>) creates new entries in the document outline. Heading content (<h1>-<h6>, <hgroup>) defines the title of a section. The outline algorithm maps heading levels relative to their sectioning root.

advanced.question: How does the <template> element differ from other HTML elements in terms of rendering?
advanced.answer: The <template> element and its content are not rendered in the DOM. Its content is stored as a DocumentFragment that can be cloned and inserted via JavaScript. This allows efficient client-side templating without affecting page load.

advanced.question: What is the purpose of the <slot> element in Web Components?
advanced.answer: The <slot> element is used within Shadow DOM to define insertion points where light DOM content can be projected. It allows web components to accept and display external content in specific positions.

advanced.question: How do custom elements extend the HTML element system?
advanced.answer: Custom elements allow developers to create new HTML tags with custom behavior using JavaScript classes. They must use a hyphenated name (e.g., <my-component>), can extend native elements, and have lifecycle callbacks (connectedCallback, attributeChangedCallback, etc.).

advanced.question: What is the difference between the <bdo> and <bdi> elements?
advanced.answer: <bdo> (Bi-Directional Override) explicitly overrides the text direction. <bdi> (Bi-Directional Isolation) isolates a span of text from its surrounding directionality, useful for handling mixed-direction text from user input.

advanced.question: How do ARIA roles relate to semantic HTML elements?
advanced.answer: ARIA roles augment or override the native semantics of HTML elements. However, using semantic HTML elements (like <nav> instead of <div role='navigation'>) is preferred because native elements have built-in accessibility features that ARIA can only partially replicate.

advanced.question: What is the <wbr> element and when should it be used?
advanced.answer:
(Word Break Opportunity) suggests a position where a line break can occur if needed. Unlike
, it only breaks when the line would otherwise overflow. It is useful for long URLs or code strings.

advanced.question: How does the browser handle unknown HTML elements?
advanced.answer: The browser treats unknown elements as generic inline elements (or block elements if they have display: block in CSS). HTML5 allows custom elements with hyphens. Older browsers may not style unknown elements correctly without JavaScript helpers.

advanced.question: What is the difference between the <embed>, <object>, and <iframe> elements?
advanced.answer: <embed> is a simple element for embedding external content (usually deprecated). <object> is more flexible and can handle various content types with fallback options. <iframe> embeds another HTML document, supporting sandboxing and seamless integration.
